Use PascalCase for all method names (e.g., CalculateTotal, GetCustomerById).
Keep methods short and focused on a single responsibility; aim for no more than 20-30 lines.
Use Function when the method returns a value and Sub when it does not; never abuse ByRef parameters as a substitute for return values.
Specify access modifiers explicitly (Public, Private, Protected, Friend) on every method.
Use ByVal (default in modern VB.NET) for parameters unless there is a specific reason to use ByRef.
Validate all public method parameters at entry (guard clauses) and throw ArgumentNullException or ArgumentException for invalid input.
Use Async/Await (Async Function ... As Task(Of T)) for I/O-bound operations instead of blocking calls.
Return Task or Task(Of T) from asynchronous methods; name them with an Async suffix (e.g., LoadDataAsync).
Extract repeated logic into well-named private helper methods to follow DRY principles.
Use interfaces (IDisposable, IComparable, etc.) and program to abstractions to support MVC/MVVM patterns and testability.
Document public methods with XML comments (''' <summary>) describing purpose, parameters, returns, and exceptions.
Prefer method overloading over Optional parameters when parameter combinations lead to significantly different behavior.
Keep the number of parameters small (ideally 3 or fewer); group related parameters into a class or structure if needed.
